coredata.dc:title: Boron nitride dots In-situ embedded in a B2O3 matrix with the long lifetime Room-Temperature phosphorescence in dry and wet states
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Shenghui
Lian, Gang
Zhang, Xu
Cao, Zhaozhen
Wang, Qilong
Cui, Deliang
Wong, Ching-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Developing metal-free room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with ultralong lifetimes in dry and wet states simultaneously has been widely concerned due to their potential applications, which is also a tough challenge up to now. Herein, a smart construction strategy is proposed to in-situ embed boron nitride dots (BNDs) into an isogenous B2O3 matrix. The BND-based materials exhibit unexpected bluish-green RTP in solid and various wet conditions (water, oxidants, organic solvents, strong acids and bases). The RTP lifetime of them in powder is as long as ~ 1.7 s, lasting over 10 s to naked eye. B2O3 matrix can not only uniformly disperse the BNDs in it, but also provide a rigid environment via forming hydrogen-bonded and covalently bonded interactions between them to strongly stabilize the triplet states excitions on the dots from nonradiative deactivation. More interestingly, RTP quenching of the BND-based powder in various solutions is effectively suppressed because the B2O3 matrix is not collapsed in them. For instance, the RTP lifetime in water is as long as ~ 0.9 s. Based on the distinctive RTP properties in dry and various harsh wet states, the applications of the BND-based RTP materials in multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption are demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron nitride dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Phosphorescence in water
Matrix
Ultralong lifetime

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BNDs@B2O3(w), BNDs and p-BNDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Key Research and Development Project (No. 51890863), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 51872172, 51972197), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018MEM010, ZR2019MEM021), Major Research and Development Program for Public Welfare in Shandong (2018GGX102021) and Young Scholars Program of Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the process of preparing the BNDs@B2O3 and pristine BNDs.

(a) and (b) TEM images of BNDs@B2O3. Inset is the size distribution of BNDs. (c) HRTEM image of individual BND. (d) TEM image of pristine BNDs obtained with the removal of B2O3. (e) XRD pattern and (f) FTIR spectrum of the BNDs@B2O3 composite. (g-i) High-resolution XPS spectra of B 1 s, N 1 s and C 1 s.

The images of the BNDs@B2O3 powder (a) and aqueous suspension (b) under daylight, UV on and UV off at room-temperature, respectively. (c) The RTP emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of BNDs@B2O3 powder. (d) The fluorescence and RTP emission spectra of BNDs@B2O3 powder under the excitation wavelengths of 280 nm. (e) Time-resolved RTP spectrum of BNDs@B2O3 powder under 450 nm with 280 nm excitation. (f) The Normalized RTP lifetime of the BNDs@B2O3 powder obtained with different mass ratio of boric acid to melamine. (g) The fluorescence and RTP emission spectra of BNDs@B2O3 suspension under the excitation wavelengths of 280 nm. (h) Time-resolved RTP spectrum of BNDs@B2O3 suspension under 450 nm with 280 nm excitation.
The photographs of BNDs@B2O3 in various oxidants, polar solvents, strong acids and bases under daylight, UV on and off.

(a) The Schematic illustration of reaction solution treated by different methods: 1. BNDs@H3BO3 (heated under 80 °C); 2. BNDs@B2O3(h) (heated under 300 °C); 3. BNDs@ B2O3(w) (heated via microwave heating); 4. d-BNDs (dialysis and freezing drying). (b) The images of samples 1, 2, 3 and 4 under daylight, UV on and UV off at room-temperature. (c) The time- resolved RTP spectra of samples 1, 2 and 3 under 450 nm with 280 nm excitation and the corresponding average lifetimes. (d) Schematic illustration of the possible chemical interaction of BNDs and B2O3 matrix.
Photographs of potential applications of the BNDs@B2O3. (a) Information display. (b) Anti-counterfeiting application. (c) Information protection. Scale bar: 1 cm.

head.ce:title: Boron nitride dots In-situ embedded in a B2O3 matrix with the long lifetime Room-Temperature phosphorescence in dry and wet states

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Crystal Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Lab of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Special Functional Aggregated Materials of Education Ministry, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Special Functional Aggregated Materials of Education Ministry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Special Functional Aggregated Materials of Education Ministry, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Georgia Institute of Technology, GA 30332, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Georgia Institute of Technology, GA 30332, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The resolution of graphical abstract has been improved.

Developing metal-free room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with ultralong lifetimes in dry and wet states simultaneously has been widely concerned due to their potential applications, which is also a tough challenge up to now. Herein, a smart construction strategy is proposed to in-situ embed boron nitride dots (BNDs) into an isogenous B2O3 matrix. The BND-based materials exhibit unexpected bluish-green RTP in solid and various wet conditions (water, oxidants, organic solvents, strong acids and bases). The RTP lifetime of them in powder is as long as ~ 1.7 s, lasting over 10 s to naked eye. B2O3 matrix can not only uniformly disperse the BNDs in it, but also provide a rigid environment via forming hydrogen-bonded and covalently bonded interactions between them to strongly stabilize the triplet states excitions on the dots from nonradiative deactivation. More interestingly, RTP quenching of the BND-based powder in various solutions is effectively suppressed because the B2O3 matrix is not collapsed in them. For instance, the RTP lifetime in water is as long as ~ 0.9 s. Based on the distinctive RTP properties in dry and various harsh wet states, the applications of the BND-based RTP materials in multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption are demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have aroused great interest due to the distinct features of long afterglow time after removing the excitation sources and large Stokes shifts, which permits their wide applications in information security, sensors, optoelectronics and bio-imaging [1–9]. Up to now, the research on RTP materials has been focusing on rare-earth-metal based inorganic materials and organometallic complexes [10–15]. However, these materials suffer from some issues, such as complex preparation, high cost, toxic effects, oxygen sensitivity, environmental hazards and low stability. Meanwhile, pure organic RTP materials could overcome these disadvantages, but poor intersystem crossing (ISC) and large nonradiative deactivation make them less efficient [15–18]. Especially, because phosphorescence is easily quenched in an aqueous solution [19], RTP feature could only be observed in their anhydrous solid state, obstructing their practical applications. Thus, it is urgent to develop a high-efficient metal-free inorganic material system through a facile strategy, requiring to exhibit ultralong RTP lifetimes in solid state and aqueous environment, respectively.

Analogous to carbon dots (CDs), boron nitride dots (BNDs), as a new class of luminescent materials, have aroused increasing attention in bio-imaging, chemical sensors, ion detection and electrochemiluminescence due to good aqueous solubility, low toxicity, excellent thermal and chemical stability [20–26]. However, these studies mainly focus on the fluorescent properties and applications of BNDs. Less attention is attracted on RTP feature of them due to poor ISC resulted from wide intrinsic bandgap of BN. Achievement of ultralong RTP lifetime is a tough challenge owing to weak spin–orbit coupling and nonradiative deactivations [27]. Recently, some CDs with RTP were realized through embedding them into protective matrices (inorganic salts, polyvinyl alcohol, zeolites, polyurethane, SiO2 etc.) [19,28–35]. The matrices can efficiently prevent the CD triplet states from aggregation-induced quenching. Inspired by this strategy, BNDs with RTP could be designed innovatively. However, most of the designed RTP dot-matrix composites are prepared via multi-step ex-situ process, determining weak interaction between host matrices and guest dots. Actually, the enhanced interaction between them can be achieved by exploring the precursors as the selection of matrices. In that case, strong interaction via covalently fixing dots onto the isogenous matrices allows enhanced spin–orbit coupling to facilitate the singlet-to-triplet ISC. Then the dot-matrix composites could break through the limitation of short RTP lifetime. Besides, almost all quantum dots present RTP only in their dry state due to water molecules destroying the hydrogen-bond skeleton and dissolved oxygen affecting the triplet state, leading to vast phosphorescence quenching [34,35]. Therefore, in order to achieve long-lived RTP emission of BNDs in wet state, it is required that the isogenous matrices are not collapsed in water first and also promote the formation of covalent and hydrogen bonds between the dots and them simultaneously. Thus the BNDs can prevent dissolved oxygen quenching and prospectively realize long RTP lifetime in water, which is critical for their applications. However, it remains a formidable challenge to achieve ultralong RTP lifetimes of BNDs in solid and wet states, respectively.

Herein, on the basis of above analysis, a smart construction strategy of BND-based materials with long afterglow in solid and wet states simultaneously is developed via in-situ embedding the BNDs into B2O3 matrix. In the reaction, boric acid (BA) is not only as the boron-source to synthesize BNDs by hydrothermal reaction, but also as the precursor to form B2O3 matrix by followed microwave-heating pyrolysis process (Fig. 1 ). In-situ composition of the BNDs in the isogenous B2O3 matrix strongly constructs covalently and hydrogen bonded interface contact, which greatly reduces nonradiative transitions. As a consequence, the as-obtained BND-based materials exhibit strong bluish-green RTP, lasting for about 10 s to the naked eye. The RTP lifetime is over 1.7 s, which is superior to the reported BNDs and most of the reported CDs. As B2O3 is not collapsed in various solutions, the solid matrix provides a rigid protection for BNDs in suspension. Then, taken their aqueous suspension for example, the RTP emission of BNDs in water was first achieved with a long lifetime of ~ 0.9 s. Based on the excellent phosphorescence response with ultralong lifetime and wet-insensitive feature, the BNDs@B2O3 RTP materials applied for multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption were successfully demonstrated.

Melamine, boric acid, isopropanol (IPA), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol, ethanol, acetone, cyclohexane, n-hexane, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetonitrile, N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), tetrahydrofuran (THF), H2O2, HNO3, H2SO4, Sodium hydroxide and urea were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. PEG 20,000 were purchased Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., LTD. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The de-ionized water was self-made in the laboratory and was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BNDs@B2O3(w), BNDs and p-BNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The BNDs were prepared by a modified method [20]. Typically, boric acid (0.45 g) was dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) to form a transparent solution, and then 0.1 g melamine was added into the boric acid aqueous solution with strongly stirring for 60 min. Then the mixture was carefully transferred into a poly(tetrafuoroethylene)-lined autoclaves for hydrothermal reaction. After heating at 220 °C for 15 h and cooling down to room temperature, the clear and transparent mixture solution was obtained. The transparent mixture solution was transferred directly into a beaker and heated in a microwave oven under 750 W for 15 min. Upon cooling down to room temperature, the white BNDs@B2O3 powder were collected. The BNDs with B2O3 removed were obtained by dialysis of BNDs@B2O3 and freeze-drying. The p-BNDs were obtained by dialysis of the solution after hydrothermal reaction and freeze-drying.
The preparation of BNDs@H3BO3 is similar to that of BNDs@B2O3, except that the final treatment is to dry the solution at 80 °C for 15 h in an oven.
The preparation of BNDs@B2O3(h) is similar to that of BNDs@B2O3, except that the final treatment is to heat the solution at 300 °C for 15 min in a muffle furnace.

The BCNO powders were prepared by a modified method [36]. Typically, boric acid (1 g) and urea (3 g) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) to form a transparent solution, and then PEG20000 (0.2 g) was added into the aqueous solution with strongly stirring for 30 min and dry. The resulting mixed powder is transferred to the crucible, and calcined in the muffle furnace at 820 °C for 30 min. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the powder was centrifuged with water for several times and dried. The BCNO powder were obtained.

The morphology and microstructure were conducted with transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL H-7000) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEM-2100). X-Ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the sample was recorded on a Rigaku D/Max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Ni filtered Cu kα radiation (V = 50 kV, I = 100 mA). The Fourier Transformation Infrared spectra (FTIR) were acquired on a Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrometer within wavenumber range 650–4000 cm−1. The element analysis was achieved by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and EDAX Genesis 4000 (EDS). Raman spectrum was collected using a Horiba LabRAM HR800 Raman spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra and room temperature phosphorescence spectra were tested via HITACH F-7000 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a 450 W Xe lamp. UV–Vis absorption spectra of powder and suspension were measured using HITACH U-2910 spectrometer and UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), respectively. The quantum yield, time resolved fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were measured using Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrometer. Photographs of fluorescence and phosphorescence were taken using a mobile phone camera under excitation by a hand-hold UV lamp (UPF200, Uvata Precision Optoelectronics Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China).

The typical procedure of preparing the BNDs@B2O3 and pristine BNDs is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. The first heating step was to synthesize BNDs by hydrothermal method. Then the water-soluble BNDs were uniformly dispersed in the clear and transparent solution with excess H3BO3. The subsequent microwave heating was to rapidly dehydrate the excess H3BO3 to form B2O3. The rapid process of water removal, including evaporation and dehydration, leaded to the BNDs embedded into the formed B2O3 matrix. The di-functional BA determines the in-situ formation of the BNDs@B2O3. The isogenous B2O3 matrix not only facilitates the uniform dispersion of BNDs in it, but also effectively protects the RTP of BNDs@B2O3 in aqueous suspension. It is noted that the BNDs@B2O3 and BNDs all emit blue fluorescence under UV excitation at 365 nm. Nevertheless, distinct difference is presented between the two powders after the removal of excitation light. Bluish-green phosphorescence can be easily recognized in the BNDs@B2O3 under ambient condition. In contrast, phosphorescence is invisible for the pristine BNDs, preliminarily demonstrating the positive effect of B2O3 matrix for BNDs RTP feature. The possible reason is that BNDs itself is a dark state system. However, the BNDs in B2O3 matrix is different from its original powder state. The strong interaction between BNDs and B2O3 matrix makes BNDs become a bright state.

The embedded feature of BNDs into B2O3 was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the quasi-spherical BNDs with a size distribution of 2–4 nm are uniformly dispersed in the B2O3 matrix. The corresponding high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image presents the distinct lattice fringes of a typical BND, indicating good crystallinity (Fig. 2c). The specific lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is matched with the (1 0 0) plane of h-BN [24,37]. For further presenting the BNDs solely, the B2O3 matrix was removed via dialysis treatment. Then uniformly quasi-spherical BNDs could be obtained in Fig. 2d, which is in accord with the result in Fig. 2b. However, after dislysis, the obtained pristine BNDs only emit strong blue fluorescence and RTP is not observed (Fig. 1). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of BNDs aqueous solution is determined to be 15.2%. Moreover, when the BA or melamine was treated via the same hydrothermal and microwave-followed approach, the obtained products all presented weak fluorescent and phosphorescent emission (Figure S1). Furthermore, after the aqueous solution of BA and melamine was treated by microwave heating directly, the as-obtained product also showed weak fluorescence and short afterglow properties (Figure S1). Therefore, it can be concluded that the strong fluorescence and emerging phosphorescence of the BNDs are realized through the assistance of B2O3 matrix. The phase composition of BNDs@B2O3 was further analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern exhibits a wide diffraction peak at ~ 25° and a sharp diffraction peak at ~ 28°, which can be indexed to be h-BN and B2O3, respectively [38,39]. A weak peak at ~ 42° should correspond to the (1 0 0) face of h-BN [37,38]. The existance of h-BN in the composition was further verified by the Raman spectrum (Figure S2).

The chemical composition and bonding mode of the BNDs@B2O3 were obtained by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2f, the FTIR spectrum of the BNDs@B2O3 exhibits an absorption band at 3000–3500 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 groups located on the surfaces of the BNDs and B2O3, which demonstrates the effective surface functionalization [24]. The characteristic peaks at 1383 and 779 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of B-N and bending vibration of B-N-B, respectively [24,40]. The sharp peak located at 1192 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetrically stretched vibration of B-O-B [4]. The absorption band at 942 cm−1 belongs to the B-C bond, indicating effective doping of carbon atoms in BNDs [4]. Actually, atomic level hybridization of carbon in BNDs is responsible for red-shifted fluorescence emission of BN [36]. The results of chemical composition and bonding identified by FTIR characterization were further verified via XPS analysis. The survey spectrum reveals that the composite is mainly composed of B, C, N and O elements (Figure S3). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of B1s can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 189.91, 190.68 and 191.95 eV (Fig. 2g), contributing to B-C, B-N and B-O bonds, respectively [41]. They are mainly derived from the carbon-doping, BNDs and B2O3, which is consistent with the FTIR results in Fig. 2f. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s shows three peaks at 398.30, 399.37, and 400.37 eV for N-B, N-C, and N–H bonds (Fig. 2h), respectively [41]. The N–H bond implies the presence of –NH2 groups. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s was fitted with three peaks at 283.76, 284.60, and 286.41 eV, corresponding to the C-B, C–C, and C-N bonds (Fig. 2i), respectively, which demonstrates the effective doping of carbon in BNDs [35]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Figure S4) contains two components, assigned to lattice oxygen (530.5 eV) and adsorbed oxygen (531.6 eV) [42,43]. Through the above analysis, it is confirmed that abundant –OH and –NH2 groups located on the surface of C-doped BNDs, which facilitates hydrogen-bonded formation on the interface of BNDs and B2O3 matrix. Partial removal of their functional groups, via dehydration reaction in microwave-pyrolyzing BA process to form B2O3 matrix, also promotes the formation of covalent bonds (B-O-B). The hydrogen-bonded and covalently coupling effects effectively lock the BNDs in the B2O3 matrix. Then the triplet excitons can be potentially activated by enhancing spin–orbit coupling and limiting vibration-caused nonradiative transition. In terms of the EDS result in Figure S5, the ratio of B, N and O elements in the BNDs@B2O3 compoiste are 34.24%, 1.72% and 56.88%, respectively. Becasue only a small amount of BNDs were embedded within the B2O3 matrix, the B and O elements are predominant in the composite, and the N element is mainly from BNDs. After the composite was dialyzed, the element composition of BNDs was further investigated by XPS (Figure S6). The ratio of B, N and O elements are 14.77%, 11.60%, and 42.81%, respectively. The B/N ratio is higher than the theoretical value, which is attributed to the residual of B2O3. The high ratio of O element in the remained sample after dislysis treatment should be derived from the residual B2O3, oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., –OH), doping of O atom in the BNDs and partial oxidation after the removal of B2O3 protection matrix.

As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, the BNDs@B2O3 powders and suspension all emit light blue fluorescence under UV excitation at 365 nm. After removal of the excitation light, bluish-green RTP can be observed by the naked eye and last for 10 s in solid state (Fig. 3a and Video S1). At low temperature (77 K), the afterglow of the BNDs@B2O3 powders can last for over 17 s, which is much longer than that at room-temperature owing to suppressing thermodynamic vibrations and rotations of emissive species (Figure S7). More importantly, the RTP emission can also be observed in their suspension. The bluish-green RTP can amazingly last for 8 s after the UV light was switched off (Fig. 3b and Video S2). Because the B2O3 was not collapsed in water, the rigid matrix can effectively prevent the phosphorescence quenching of external factors (i.e., dissolved oxygen and water molecules) on triplet excitons of the embedded BNDs. In contrast, for the pristine BNDs and BNDs@H3BO3 aqueous solution, direct exposure of BNDs and collapse of H3BO3 matrix in water, respectively, cause RTP of them disappearing (Figure S8). Thus, the RTP property of BNDs in wet state is first demonstrated. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of BNDs@B2O3 composite could remain over 95% of their initial value after dispersing them in a considerably tough aqueous environment of 0.2 M NaCl (Figure S9a). The BNDs@B2O3 suspension in 0.2 M NaCl aqueous solution also exhibits high photostability (Figure S9b).

It is known that RTP phenomenon generally undergoes extensive quenching in aqueous suspension. Accordingly, no phosphorescence was observed in the aqueous suspension of pristine BNDs and BNDs@H3BO3, respectively, due to lack of rigid protection (Figure S8). B2O3 without collapse in water is expected a potential matrix to load BNDs. It can be easily obtained by the pyrolysis of unreacted BA precursor. Then the in-situ composition of BNDs in B2O3 matrix can be realized. In addition, because a large number of –OH and –NH2 functional groups exist on the surfaces of hydrothermally synthesized BNDs, they can covalently bond with B2O3 via dehydration process of BA. Combining of the inter-/intra-dot hydrogen bonds, the obtained BNDs@B2O3 powder showed an afterglow in an aqueous environment. The fluorescence and RTP emission of BNDs@B2O3 aqueous dispersion are shown in Fig. 3g. Compared to the fluorescence emisison of BNDs@B2O3 in water, that in solid state is red-shifted, which is related with energy resonance transfer and fulorescence self-absorption in the aggregated powder. Similar to the emission in powder, the RTP feature of BNDs@B2O3 aqueous suspension is also excitation-dependent with an optimal excitation light of 280 nm (Figure S12). The corresponding RTP emission is located at 450 nm, which is consistent with that in powder, confirming well maintained RTP properties in wet state. Up to now, an effectively RTP phenomenon based on BNDs in water is hardly reported. Furthermore, the corresponding time-resolved RTP decay spectrum was also investigated in Fig. 3h. Surprisingly, the BNDs@B2O3 aqueous suspension has an ultralong RTP lifetime of 899.75 ms according to Table S4. The ultralong lifetime in solid and wet states should be attributed to the two reasons. One of the origin comes from the doping of heteroatoms and defect formation. They can enhance the spin–orbit coupling and reduce the energy gap between the lowest excited singlet state and triplet state, promting the efficiency of intersystem crossing. Another origin is due to the dispersion and stabilization of BNDs by B2O3 matrix. B2O3 matrix can form covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds interaction with BNDs to stablize triple excited states. The composition structure can also prevent triple excited states from quenching by water and oxygen molecules. The shortening of RTP lifetime from ~ 1.7 s in powder to ~ 900 ms in aqueous suspension should be attributed to partial destruction of hydrogen bonds between dots and matrix in water. In addition, the BNDs@B2O3 in powder and aqueous suspension all exhibits high photostability (Figure S13 and S14).

In addition, in view of the complex liquid application circumstance, the anti-quenching properties of BNDs@B2O3 sample were also investigated in other solvents and solutions. As shown in Fig. 4 , the BNDs@B2O3 sample was first dispersed in strong oxidants, including H2O2 (30 wt%), concentrated HNO3 (65 wt%), and H2SO4 (98 wt%). Surprisingly, both the fluorescence and RTP are not quenched by strong oxidants, and afterglow can still be observed with the naked eye. Furthermore, when the BNDs@B2O3 powder was dispersed in different polar organic solvents, the phosphors also maintained excellent stability in them respectively. To further highlight the anti-quenching ability, the BNDs@B2O3 powder was dispersed in aqueous solutions at pH values varying from 1.0 to 12.0. The RTP emission can still be observed by the naked eye in the pH range of 1.0 to 12.0, which indicates that the BNDs@B2O3 phosphors can resist strong acid and base. The stability investigation confirms that the phosphors can maintain good RTP feature in acid and base for one day (Figure S15). Because the B2O3 matrix slowly dissovles in acid and alkaline solutions, the RTP eimission gradually disappears after 24 h. In contrat, the composite without obvious collapse in water exhibits extremely stable RTP emission even after 7 days. These results demonstrate the strong protecting effect of the B2O3 matrix on the triplet state of BNDs in wet state. Meanwhile, the BNDs@B2O3 phosphors exhibit an excellent stability, determining them suitable for various harsh environments.

To illustrate the effect of in-situ incorporating BNDs into isogenous B-based matrices for long RTP lifetime, we further designed a series of composites. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after the hydrothermal reaction for synthesizing BNDs was proceeded, different from the BNDs@B2O3(w) powder obtained via microwave heating (route 3), the BNDs@H3BO3 and BNDs@B2O3(h) products were prepared by directly heating the solution at 80 (route 1) and 300 °C (route 2) to remove the residual water, respectively. The corresponding TEM images (Figures S16 and S17) illustrate the BNDs successfully embedded into the B-based matrices. Similar to the luminescent features of BNDs@B2O3(w) powder, the two composites also exhibit blue and bluish-green RTP (Fig. 5b). In addition, the pristine BNDs without matrix could be obtained by dialysis (route 4, denoted as d-BNDs). Unfortunately, RTP emission could not be observed after UV lamp switching off, indicating the matrix effect to activate RTP of BNDs. However, there is an obvious difference of afterglow time of three composites (Fig. 5b). Based on the phosphorescence decay curves and fitting of bi-exponential function, the average RTP lifetimes of them in powder were listed in Fig. 5c. It is clear that the BNDs@H3BO3 composite presents a relative short lifetime of 1.077 s due to weak interaction between BNDs and matrix (Table S5). In contrast, the strong interaction, including hydrogen and covalent bonds, were expectedly formed between BNDs and B2O3 via high-temperature treatment at 300 °C, constructing a rigid environment to limit the nonradiative decay of the triplet excitions and promote ISC [46,47]. Thus the obtained BNDs@B2O3(h) composite exhibits a relative long lifetime of 1.348 s (Table S5). Unfortunately, sintering the composite at 300 °C could partially remove the –OH and –NH2 groups from the dot surfaces, thereby affecting the RTP lifetime. It is due to the important role of inter-/intra-dot hydrogen bonds in solidifying the triplet state and suppressing the nonradiation transitions of triplet excitons [48]. In addition, high temperature could also consume the emissive species (defects and doped carbon) partially, affecting the optical properties. Accordingly, a mild microwave-heating approach to avoid severe elimination of the –OH and –NH2 groups from the dot surfaces (Figure S18) is proposed for the formation of BNDs@B2O3(w). The corresponding RTP lifetime is as high as 1.704 s, which is much longer than these of other two composites. The possible explanations are listed as follows. First, the doping of C element in the BN lattice potentially reduces the energy gap between singlet and triplet states, facilitating ISC occurrence. The chemical structure illustration of C-doped BNDs is shown in Figure S19. Second, the abundant functional groups (–OH and –NH2) on the surface of BNDs is favorable for forming hydrogen-bonded interaction between BNDs and B2O3 matrix (Fig. 5d). Combining of inter-/intra-dot hydrogen bonds interaction, a rigid environment is constructed to lock the BNDs in the matrix, which stabilizes the triplet excitons and limits the nonradiative decay of them. Third, in-situ composition can uniformly disperse the BNDs into the matrix. It is speculated that dehydration occurring on the interfaces of OH–/NH2-terminated BNDs and H3BO3 can link the dots on the BN/B2O3 interfaces by a covalent bond (···B-O-B···). The strong interaction makes the constructed hydrogen-bond skeleton more stable and limits the vibration and rotation of chromophores in BNDs[47,48]. Thus a transition of BNDs from dark state (in powder state) to bright state (in B2O3 matrix) was achieved. Based on the analysis and discussion of the experimental results, a possible mechanism of the fluorescence and ultralong RTP emission of BNDs@B2O3 is presented in Figure S20.

Given the excellent feature of BNDs@B2O3 composite, its potential application in information encryption and anti-counterfeit technology was demonstrated. Correspondingly, BNDs@H3BO3 with short RTP lifetime and BCNO without phosphorescence emission were used as interferences. As shown in Fig. 6 a, we simply designed a graphic pattern “rose”. The leaves, branches and petals are constructed of BCNO, BNDs@H3BO3 and BNDs@B2O3, respectively. A bright blue “rose” appears due to the fluorescence emission of them under the excitation of 365 nm light. After switching off the excitation light, a clearly bluish-green “rose” without leaves shows up. Because of the remarkable difference of afterglow time between the BNDs@H3BO3 and BNDs@B2O3, the phosphorescence pattern can last for several seconds. Then only the petals are clearly visible 5 s later. So far, most reported RTP materials have failed to achieve phosphorescence emission in wet state. Impressively, only bright bluish-green petals can be recognized after spraying water on the “rose” pattern, which demonstrates that the BNDs@B2O3 composite can be used as an advanced multi-level anti-counterfeiting material for information encryption based on the fluorescence, long afterglow phosphorescence and water-resistant phosphorescence. Inspired of the result, BNDs@B2O3 as a novel information encryption material was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 6b, a code “123” was designed using the BNDs@H3BO3, BCNO, and BNDs@B2O3, respectively, where the genuine code “3” was encrypted by BNDs@B2O3. After the UV lamp is switched off, the number “13” can be observed due to the RTP properties of BNDs@H3BO3 and BNDs@B2O3. However, the code “13” is fake because of interfering materials of BNDs@H3BO3. If we want to decrypt the correct code, it is required to spray water on the pattern because the phosphorescence emission of BNDs@H3BO3 can be quenched by water. Thereby, only genuine code “3” encrypted by BNDs@B2O3 can be observed in phosphorescence mode. In addition, the anti-counterfeiting of confidential document using the aqueous suspension of BNDs@B2O3 was also demonstrated (Fig. 6c). In daylight, the confidential information can not be recognized by the naked eye in the document. After the removal of UV lamp, the bluish-green confidential information “true” appears, which is free from the interference of background fluorescence. More precisely, the authenticity of the document can be identified according to the RTP emission wavelength or lifetime. Actually, the BNDs@B2O3 can be potentially applied in the anti-counterfeiting of currency and precious products.

In summary, an effective approach to activate RTP of BNDs in dry and wet states has been proposed via in-situ embedding the dots into an isogenous B2O3 matrix. The BNDs are uniformly locked into the matrix. The hydrogen-bonded and covalently bonded interactions between BNDs and B2O3 can strongly stabilize the triplet excitons on the BNDs from nonradiative deactivation. The rigid matrix without collapse in water and various liquid conditions (strong oxidants, polar organic solvents, strong acids and bases) can effectively suppress the phosphorescence quenching of external factors (i.e., dissolved oxygen and water molecules) on the triplet excitons of embedded BNDs. Therefore, the RTP lifetimes of BND-based materials in powder and aqueous suspension reach as high as ~ 1.7 and ~ 0.9 s, respectively, which is much longer than most reported BND- and CD-based RTP materials. Furthermore, the BND-based materials can be applied in advanced multi-level anti-counterfeiting and information encryption fields owing to their distinctive RTP performance in dry and wet states.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Key Research and Development Project (No. 51890863), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 51872172, 51972197), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018MEM010, ZR2019MEM021), Major Research and Development Program for Public Welfare in Shandong (2018GGX102021) and Young Scholars Program of Shandong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129175.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excited state modulation for organic afterglow: Materials and applications
